title: India-Nepal Bilateral Expansion: EAM Jaishankar Signals Pivot to AI, Startups, Clean Energy
lede: External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar welcomed Nepal's Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal to Delhi on June 6, 2026, signaling a decisive expansion of the bilateral relationship beyond traditional energy cooperation into artificial intelligence, information technology, renewable energy, and startups.

sections.context: The meeting between EAM Jaishankar and Foreign Minister Khanal occurred just two months after their previous engagement in Mauritius, underscoring the high tempo of India-Nepal diplomatic engagement. Jaishankar's opening remarks emphasized that the bilateral relationship rests on foundations of people-to-people ties, cross-border connectivity, and shared cultural and religious traditions, anchored by what he characterized as "shared trust, goodwill and mutual benefit." The timing of this visit reflects sustained Indian attention to its Himalayan neighbor, with the EAM noting that existing bilateral cooperation has steadily expanded across traditional sectors including trade, commerce, investments, energy, development cooperation, education, disaster response, and culture. The Foreign Minister's visit to India provides a platform to articulate what Jaishankar framed as an opportunity to advance cooperation "in newer domains like startups, AI, Information Technology, renewable energy, and others."
sections.threads: This expansion into emerging technology sectors signals a [[india-np-india-and-nepal-expand-bilateral-agenda-beyond-energy-i-2026|deliberate pivot beyond energy as the primary driver of bilateral ties]], a traditional centerpiece of India-Nepal cooperation. The emphasis on artificial intelligence and information technology also reflects broader regional patterns of technological competition and the need for India to anchor its Himalayan partnerships in 21st-century domains. Notably, Jaishankar emphasized the security dimension of the relationship, stating that the two countries "collaborate closely to maintain security along our long and uniquely open border," a reference to the distinctive characteristics of the India-Nepal frontier. The EAM also highlighted India's supply of uninterrupted fuel to Nepal amid the ongoing Middle East crisis, demonstrating how India calibrates its strategic support during periods of regional instability and positioning energy security as both a humanitarian and geopolitical lever.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor whether the stated ambition to expand into AI, startups, and renewable energy translates into concrete institutional mechanisms, joint projects, or financial commitments in the coming 6-18 months. The next decision point will likely involve whether India and Nepal establish formal cooperation frameworks or dedicated initiatives in these emerging sectors, similar to the structures they have built for hydropower and traditional development cooperation. Close attention should be paid to how [[india-np-nepal-fm-in-delhi-mlaa-entry-into-force-marks-instituti-2026|institutional mechanisms such as bilateral agreements]] evolve to accommodate these newer domains. Additionally, watch for whether the emphasis on renewable energy cooperation signals a reorientation of energy partnership away from hydropower toward solar, wind, or cross-border grid modernization. The stated commitment to border security and fuel supply continuity suggests India views Nepal as critical to its regional energy strategy and security architecture.
